nativeRange.habitat: Coastal sand flats, sand slopes and deep lagoons adjacent to reef structure, typically at depths of 3-35 m. Observed hovering at the entrance of a shared pistol-shrimp burrow on fine sand or coral rubble.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5-8 cm deep for burrowing
loose coral rubble or shell fragments for the partner shrimp to build with
open sandy footprint near the front for visibility
low to moderate flow over the burrow zone
scattered live rock structure for cover

diet.notes: Carnivorous; generally accepts a varied meaty diet of frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped table shrimp, krill, and small carnivore pellets. Best fed at least twice daily in small portions. Newly imported specimens have been reported to be shy at first and may need targeted feeding near the burrow until they settle.

goodWith: pistol shrimp (Alpheus randalli — preferred symbiotic partner)
small peaceful reef fish (firefish, cardinalfish, royal grammas)
avoidWith: conspecifics (territorial toward other Amblyeleotris yanoi unless a bonded pair)
other shrimp gobies competing for the same substrate territory
large aggressive predators (groupers, lionfish, large angelfish, large hawkfish)
triggerfish and other crustacean-hunting species that would target the partner shrimp
very small ornamental shrimp (Sexy Shrimp, very small Periclimenes) that could be eaten

summary: A medium-sized burrowing shrimp goby with a pale body, pinkish-orange bars and a striking yellow flag-shaped caudal fin. Forms a symbiotic burrow partnership with Randall's pistol shrimp on sandy reef slopes.
careNotes:
The Flagtail Shrimp Goby is one of the larger Amblyeleotris shrimp gobies in the hobby — males have been reported to reach around 13 cm, while females tend to stay considerably smaller (around 5-6 cm). Because of this sexual size dimorphism a pair will look noticeably mismatched, with the male standing sentry over the burrow entrance while the smaller female stays closer to cover.

Like most members of its genus, A. yanoi naturally pairs with a pistol shrimp — most often Randall's Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus randalli), which excavates and maintains a shared burrow while the goby acts as a lookout. Tiger Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus bellulus) are reported as an acceptable alternative partner in captivity. To support this behaviour, a fine sand bed of at least 5-8 cm with a few flat rubble pieces for the shrimp to build under is generally advised.

Cross-source disagreement notes: temperature recommendations span LiveAquaria 22-26°C (72-78°F), reef-care databases ~26°C, and FishBase 27-29°C from wild data — the recorded 24-28°C range averages these. Tank-size guidance also varies (LiveAquaria 30 gal / ~114 L, reefapp.net 100 L, AquaInfo 100 L minimum but 200 L preferred); the listed 150 L minimum biases toward the larger figure because the male can reach ~13 cm, the species is described as territorial toward conspecifics, and a longer footprint provides a more stable burrow territory.

A tightly fitting lid is important — like most gobies and dartfish, this species has been known to jump when startled. The species is generally considered fully reef-safe and ignores corals, but very small ornamental shrimp could be at risk and conspecifics should be avoided unless deliberately bonded as a pair.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is poorly documented. Like related Amblyeleotris and Stonogobiops shrimp gobies, pairs are presumed to spawn inside the shared burrow with the male tending the eggs; rearing the pelagic larvae would likely require very small live foods (rotifers, then copepod nauplii) and is the main hurdle for hobbyist breeding success.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary hobby source for water parameters, minimum tank size (30 gal), reef safety, temperament, care level (Easy) and diet recommendations. The original manifest URL (the gobies collection root) did not surface this species directly; the specific product page was located via search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), wild distribution (Ryukyu Islands to Papua New Guinea), depth range (3-35 m), sexual size dimorphism (males to 13 cm SL, females to 5.8 cm SL), wild temperature range (26.9-29°C), and confirmation of Alpheus randalli as the symbiotic partner shrimp.

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (100 L minimum, larger preferred), 13 cm max size, territorial/jumping behaviour, partner shrimp requirement, and reef-safe status. Cited as confirming source for the larger-male body size that drove the 150 L minimum decision.

additional.name: AquaInfo
additional.notes: Third hobby source confirming 100 L minimum (with 200 L preferred), 27-29°C temperature range, pH 8.1-8.4, carnivorous diet, symbiosis with Alpheus randalli, and pair/solitary social structure. Wikipedia (limited stub) was consulted only to confirm taxonomy and binomial authority.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning behaviour in captivity is poorly documented; like related shrimp gobies the male is presumed to tend a clutch inside the burrow, with pelagic larvae that need very small live first foods.
